IProcessedMessageStore)       │   │\\n                │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\n                │    │                                                      │\\n                │    │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │\\n                │    │  │  BRIDGE COMPONENT (Standard Architecture)    │   │\\n                │    │  │  • BridgeConsumer (Kafka→RabbitMQ bridge)    │   │\\n                │    │  │    (manual idempotency, not base class)      │   │\\n                │    │  │  • Part of dual-broker event distribution    │   │\\n                │    │  │                                              │   │\\n                │    │  │  RabbitMQ Broker Middleware Stack:           │   │\\n                │    │  │  • CircuitBreakerMiddleware (resilience)     │   │\\n                │    │  │  • RateLimiterMiddleware (optional)          │   │\\n                │    │  │  • RabbitPrometheusMiddleware (metrics)      │   │\\n                │    │  │  • TelemetryMiddleware (OTel, shared)        │   │\\n                 │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\n                 │    │                                  │ On failure        │\\n                 │    │                                  ▼                   │\\n                 │    │                        ┌──────────────────┐          │\\n                 │    │                        │ EXTERNAL DLQ     │          │\\n                 │    │                        │ (broker native)  │          │\\n                 │    │                        │ • Kafka Connect  │          │\\n                 │    │                        │   DLQ SMT        │          │\\n                 │    │                        │ • RabbitMQ DLX   │          │\\n                 │    │                        └─────────┬────────┘          │\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\n                 │    │                                  ▼                   │\\n                 │    │                        ┌──────────────────┐          │\\n                 │    │                        │  DLQ Bookkeeper  │          │\\n                 │    │                        │  Consumer        │          │\\n                 │    │                        │  (@broker.sub)   │          │\\n                 │    │                        └──────────────────┘          │\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\n                 └────┼──────────────────────────────────┘                   │\\n                      │                                                      │\\n                      └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\\n                                                 │\\n                                                 ▼\\n                                    ┌─────────────────────┐\\n                                    │  Admin/Ops API      │\\n                                    │  • DLQAdminService  │\\n                                    │  • Replay API       │\\n                                    │  • Health Checks    │\\n                                    └─────────────────────┘\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n## FastStream: The Broker Adapter\\n\\n[FastStream](https://faststream.ag2.ai/) is the **Pythonic adapter** that wraps low-level Kafka/RabbitMQ clients (like &#96;confluent-kafka&#96;, &#96;aio-pika&#96;) and provides a decorator-based, async/await, type-safe API.\\n\\n**Think of it like SQLAlchemy for message brokers** - it hides 200+ lines of boilerplate:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\n# WITHOUT FastStream (raw Kafka client)\\nfrom confluent_kafka import Consumer\\nconsumer = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092', ...})\\nconsumer.subscribe(['user.created'])\\nwhile True:\\n    msg = consumer.poll(1.0)\\n    if msg:\\n        event_dict = json.loads(msg.value().decode())\\n        event = UserCreatedEvent(**event_dict)  # Manual conversion\\n        await handle_user_created(event)\\n        consumer.commit(msg)\\n\\n# WITH FastStream (what you actually write)\\nfrom faststream.confluent import KafkaBroker\\n\\nbroker = KafkaBroker(\\\"localhost:9092\\\")\\n\\n@broker.subscriber(\\\"user.created\\\")\\nasync def handle_user_created(event: UserCreatedEvent):  # ✅ Already a Pydantic model!\\n    # FastStream automatically:\\n    # - Polls Kafka, decodes bytes → JSON → Pydantic\\n    # - Runs middleware (telemetry, circuit breaker, rate limiter)\\n    # - Commits offset on success, sends to DLQ on failure\\n    print(f\\\"User {event.user_id} created!\\\")\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**What FastStream provides:**\\n\\n- 🎯 **Decorator-based subscriptions**: &#96;@broker.subscriber(\\\"topic\\\")&#96;\\n- 🔄 **Automatic Pydantic conversion**: Type hints → auto-deserialization\\n- 🔌 **Middleware hooks**: Circuit breakers, rate limiting, telemetry, metrics\\n- 🏥 **Native health checks**: &#96;make_ping_asgi&#96; endpoint for broker status\\n- 🔀 **Unified API**: Same code works for Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS, Redis Streams\\n\\n&#96;python-eventing&#96; uses FastStream for all Kafka/RabbitMQ interactions, giving you a clean, Pythonic API while handling all the low-level complexity.\\n\\n## Setup\\n\\n### Database schema\\n\\nThe outbox table stores events transactionally with your business data:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;sql\\nCREATE TABLE outbox_events (\\n    event_id VARCHAR(36) PRIMARY KEY,\\n    event_type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\n    aggregate_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\n    payload JSONB NOT NULL,\\n    occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\n    published BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\n    failed BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\n    attempt_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL,\\n    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\n    published_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\n    failed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\n    error_message TEXT\\n);\\n\\nCREATE INDEX idx_outbox_unpublished ON outbox_events (published, created_at);\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Application startup\\n\\nInitialize the outbox repository and event bus at application startup:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI\\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\nfrom messaging.core import build_event_bus\\n\\napp = FastAPI()\\n\\n@app.on_event(\\\"startup\\\")\\nasync def startup():\\n    # Database engine\\n    engine = create_async_engine(\\\"postgresql+asyncpg://...\\\")\\n    \\n    # Outbox repository\\n    outbox_repo = SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository(engine)\\n    app.state.outbox_repository = outbox_repo\\n    \\n    # Optional: EventBus for advanced patterns\\n    event_bus = build_event_bus()\\n    app.state.event_bus = event_bus\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**CDC Publishing:** Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC automatically detects outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. See [&#96;debezium-cdc-architecture.md&#96;](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/debezium-cdc-architecture.html) for configuration.\\n\\n## Quick Start: Transactional Outbox\\n\\nThe **core pattern** is the transactional outbox - persist events atomically with your business data:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom fastapi import Depends\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\n\\n# Define domain event\\nclass UserCreated(BaseEvent):\\n    event_type: str = \\\"user.created\\\"\\n    aggregate_id: str\\n    user_id: int\\n    email: str\\n\\n# Simple, direct approach (recommended)\\n@app.post(\\\"/users\\\")\\nasync def create_user(\\n    data: CreateUserRequest,\\n    session = Depends(get_session),\\n    outbox_repo: SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository = Depends(get_outbox_repo)\\n):\\n    # 1. Business logic\\n    user = User(**data.dict())\\n    session.add(user)\\n\\n    # 2. Persist event to outbox (same transaction)\\n    await outbox_repo.add_event(\\n        UserCreated(\\n            aggregate_id=f\\\"user-{user.id}\\\",\\n            user_id=user.id,\\n            email=user.email,\\n        ),\\n        session=session\\n    )\\n\\n    # 3. Commit both atomically\\n    await session.commit()\\n\\n    # 4. Kafka Connect (Debezium CDC) detects the outbox insert and publishes to Kafka\\n    return {\\\"user_id\\\": user.id}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**Result**: Guaranteed delivery, no lost events, atomic writes.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Advanced: EventBus (Optional)\\n\\nFor **decoupled architectures** with multiple side effects per event, use the **EventBus** abstraction layer:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import OutboxEventHandler\\n\\n# Access EventBus (initialized at startup)\\nevent_bus = request.app.state.event_bus\\noutbox_repo = request.app.state.outbox_repository\\n\\n# Register handler (typically at startup)\\nevent_bus.register(UserCreated, OutboxEventHandler(outbox_repo))\\n\\n# Dispatch (same result as direct add_event, but decoupled)\\nawait event_bus.dispatch(UserCreated(...))\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**When to use EventBus**:\\n\\n- ✅ Multiple side effects per event (audit, metrics, cache)\\n- ✅ Need lifecycle hooks for observability\\n- ✅ Testing isolation (enable/disable toggle)\\n- ✅ Decorator-based handler registration\\n\\n**When NOT needed**:\\n\\n- ❌ Simple event persistence (use direct &#96;outbox_repo.add_event()&#96;)\\n- ❌ Single handler per event\\n- ❌ No need for hooks/tracing\\n\\n📚 **[EventBus Documentation](./docs/eventbus/usage-guide.md)** - Complete guide for advanced patterns\\n\\n## Distribution\\n\\n- PyPI distribution name: &#96;python-eventing&#96;\\n- Python import package: &#96;messaging&#96;\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\n# Install\\npip install python-eventing\\n\\n# Import\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nServices should consume the published package rather than source checkout. Kafka remains shared infrastructure with local producer/consumer clients per service.\\n\\n## Local development\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\\npoetry install\\npoetry build\\npoetry run pytest\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Testing\\n\\n**Local (Windows):**\\n- Run non-RabbitMQ tests: &#96;poetry run pytest tests/ -v -m \\\"not requires_rabbitmq\\\"&#96;\\n- RabbitMQ integration tests fail due to Docker Desktop networking limitations\\n\\n**CI/CD (Recommended for complete coverage):**\\n- GitHub Actions runs all 192 tests on Linux (100% pass rate)\\n- Workflows configured in &#96;.github/workflows/&#96;\\n- See &#96;.github/workflows/README.md&#96; for details\\n\\n**For complete local test coverage on Windows:** See [Windows Testing with WSL2](docs/WINDOWS_TESTING_WITH_WSL2.md) (requires Docker Desktop WSL integration setup).\\n\\n\"}}"
}

(distribution is &#96;python-eventing&#96;)\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**Requirements:**\\\\n- Python 3.12+\\\\n- PostgreSQL (outbox persistence)\\\\n- Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC (publishing infrastructure)\\\\n\\\\n## When to use this package\\\\n\\\\n**Use &#96;python-eventing&#96; if you need:**\\\\n- Guaranteed event delivery via transactional outbox pattern\\\\n- Kafka-based microservice messaging with CDC publishing\\\\n- Dead letter queue handling with database bookkeeping\\\\n- Idempotent consumer patterns with durable deduplication\\\\n- Native broker integration (FastStream, Debezium CDC, RabbitMQ DLX)\\\\n\\\\n**Consider alternatives if:**\\\\n- Simple in-process events only → [&#96;pyventus&#96;](https://github.com/mdapena/pyventus)\\\\n- FastAPI request-scoped events → [&#96;fastapi-events&#96;](https://github.com/melvinkcx/fastapi-events)\\\\n- Non-Kafka message brokers without CDC support\\\\n- No need for durable outbox persistence\\\\n\\\\nSupport scale: &#96;❌&#96; none, &#96;✅&#96; basic, &#96;✅✅&#96; strong, &#96;✅✅✅&#96; first-class\\\\n\\\\n## Comparison with alternatives\\\\n\\\\n&#96;python-eventing&#96; prioritizes **durable messaging** (transactional outbox + CDC) and **Kafka/RabbitMQ integration** over in-process event simplicity:\\\\n\\\\n| Capability | &#96;python-eventing&#96; | [&#96;pyventus&#96;](https://github.com/mdapena/pyventus) | [&#96;fastapi-events&#96;](https://github.com/melvinkcx/fastapi-events) | Notes |\\\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\\\n| Transactional outbox | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Durable local DB plus outbox boundary is a core feature here |\\\\n| Kafka data plane | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | This package is built for Kafka-backed microservice messaging |\\\\n| DLQ handling | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Leverages native RabbitMQ DLX and Kafka Connect DLQ SMT with database bookkeeping |\\\\n| Health checks for eventing runtime | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Outbox health checks plus FastStream ASGI broker health endpoint |\\\\n| Typed cross-service event contracts | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;python-eventing&#96; and &#96;fastapi-events&#96; are stronger on explicit payload modeling |\\\\n| Decorator subscriber registration | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;EventBus.subscriber(...)&#96; exists now; &#96;pyventus&#96; is still the most polished here |\\\\n| In-process dispatch backend abstraction | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchBackend&#96; exists here; &#96;pyventus&#96; offers a broader processor model |\\\\n| Lifecycle hooks / callbacks | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchHooks&#96; covers dispatch, success, failure, disabled, and debug |\\\\n| Debug / disable controls | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;DispatchSettings(enabled, debug)&#96; is implemented; &#96;fastapi-events&#96; is strongest for app-level toggling |\\\\n| Observability / telemetry polish | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | FastStream native middlewares (KafkaTelemetryMiddleware, KafkaPrometheusMiddleware) integrated |\\\\n| Resilience middleware | ✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | CircuitBreakerMiddleware (prevents cascading failures) and RateLimiterMiddleware (throttles consumption rate) |\\\\n| CDC-based outbox publishing | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC handles outbox-to-Kafka publishing |\\\\n| Consumer dedup helper | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IdempotentConsumerBase&#96; now uses a durable processed-message store instead of process memory |\\\\n| Durable cross-service idempotency | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IProcessedMessageStore&#96; plus &#96;SqlAlchemyProcessedMessageStore&#96; provide transactional duplicate protection |\\\\n| Consumer batch handling | ❌ | ❌ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;fastapi-events&#96; supports &#96;handle_many(...)&#96;; this package stays one-message-per-consume today |\\\\n| FastAPI-local event flow | ❌ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | This package intentionally avoids request-lifecycle middleware eventing |\\\\n\\\\n## Scope\\\\n\\\\n**Included:**\\\\n- Transactional outbox primitives (write-side only; CDC handles publishing)\\\\n- Event contracts and registry\\\\n- Kafka/RabbitMQ consumer base classes with idempotency\\\\n- Native broker integration (Kafka Connect CDC, RabbitMQ DLX, FastStream middlewares)\\\\n- In-process emitter/subscriber facade and hooks\\\\n- DLQ bookkeeping consumer for database flag synchronization\\\\n\\\\n**NOT included (delegated to external systems):**\\\\n- Event publishing (handled by Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC)\\\\n- Message broker infrastructure setup (use official Kafka/RabbitMQ documentation)\\\\n- Schema registry management (use Confluent Schema Registry or alternatives)\\\\n- Request-scoped FastAPI event middleware (intentionally avoided)\\\\n- Consumer batch handling (use &#96;fastapi-events&#96; if needed)\\\\n\\\\n## Documentation\\\\n\\\\n📖 **[Integration Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/integration-guide.html)** - Step-by-step integration instructions\\\\n\\\\n🔍 **[API Reference](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/index.html)** - Complete API documentation\\\\n\\\\n📋 **[Event Catalog](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/event-catalog.html)** - Available event types and contracts\\\\n\\\\n### Key topics\\\\n\\\\n- [Transactional Outbox Pattern](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/transactional-outbox.html) - Guaranteed event delivery (PRIMARY)\\\\n- [Cross-Service Communication](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html) - Database isolation, Kafka/RabbitMQ architecture, production deployment\\\\n- [Idempotent Consumers](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/consumer-transactions.html) - Duplicate message handling\\\\n- [Health Checks](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/eventing/infrastructure/health/index.html) - Monitoring outbox and broker status\\\\n\\\\n**Architecture note**: This package handles the **write side** of the outbox pattern (persisting events transactionally with business data). **Publishing** is delegated to Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC, which captures outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. The **bridge component** (part of standard architecture) forwards events from Kafka to RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP. Dead letter handling leverages native broker mechanisms (RabbitMQ DLX, Kafka Connect DLQ SMT) with a minimal bookkeeping consumer to maintain database failed-event flags.\\\\n\\\\n## Architecture\\\\n\\\\n### Quick Overview\\\\n\\\\n**Cross-service communication pattern:** Each microservice has its own PostgreSQL database (database-per-service). Services communicate via **Kafka** (shared event backbone) and **RabbitMQ** (dual-broker pattern via bridge).\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\\\\nflowchart LR\\\\n    subgraph Service_A[\\\\\\\"Service A (Producer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        AppA[FastAPI Route]\\\\n        DB1[(PostgreSQL A\u003cbr/\u003eoutbox_events)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Infrastructure[\\\\\\\"Shared Infrastructure\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        CDC[Kafka Connect CDC]\\\\n        Kafka([Apache Kafka\u003cbr/\u003eTopic: events])\\\\n        Bridge[Eventing Bridge\u003cbr/\u003eKafka → RabbitMQ]\\\\n        RMQ([RabbitMQ\u003cbr/\u003eExchange: events])\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Service_B[\\\\\\\"Service B (Kafka Consumer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        Handler1[Event Handler]\\\\n        DB2[(PostgreSQL B\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Service_C[\\\\\\\"Service C (RabbitMQ Consumer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        Handler2[Event Handler]\\\\n        DB3[(PostgreSQL C\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    DLQ([DLQ: Native Broker])\\\\n\\\\n    %% Primary Path (Kafka)\\\\n    AppA --\u003e|1. Atomic Write| DB1\\\\n    DB1 -.-\u003e|2. CDC Monitor| CDC\\\\n    CDC --\u003e|3. Publish| Kafka\\\\n    Kafka --\u003e|4. Subscribe| Handler1\\\\n    Handler1 --\u003e|5. Dedup Check| DB2\\\\n\\\\n    %% Bridge Path (RabbitMQ)\\\\n    Kafka -.-\u003e|6. Bridge Forward| Bridge\\\\n    Bridge --\u003e|7. Publish| RMQ\\\\n    RMQ -.-\u003e|8. Subscribe| Handler2\\\\n    Handler2 --\u003e|9. Dedup Check| DB3\\\\n\\\\n    %% Error Flow\\\\n    Handler1 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\\\n    Handler2 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\\\n\\\\n    %% Styling\\\\n    classDef app fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef db fill:#2196F3,stroke:#1565C0,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef broker fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef infra fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef error fill:#F44336,stroke:#C62828,color:#fff\\\\n\\\\n    class AppA,Handler1,Handler2 app\\\\n    class DB1,DB2,DB3 db\\\\n    class Kafka,RMQ broker\\\\n    class CDC,Bridge infra\\\\n    class DLQ error\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**Key points:**\\\\n\\\\n- Each service has **separate PostgreSQL database** (DB1, DB2, DB3)\\\\n- **Kafka** is the shared event bus connecting all services\\\\n- **Kafka Connect CDC** watches Service A's outbox and publishes to Kafka\\\\n- **Service B** consumes directly from Kafka\\\\n- **Bridge** forwards Kafka → RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP\\\\n- **Service C** consumes from RabbitMQ\\\\n- Both Kafka and RabbitMQ are part of the **standard architecture**\\\\n\\\\n**Guarantees:**\\\\n\\\\n- **Write Phase**: ✅ Atomic (business data + event in same transaction, same database)\\\\n- **Publish Phase**: ✅ At-least-once (CDC retries on failure)\\\\n- **Consume Phase**: ✅ Exactly-once (idempotency via processed message store in consumer's database)\\\\n\\\\n📖 **[Cross-Service Communication Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html)** - Detailed explanation with production deployment patterns\\\\n\\\\n### Database isolation and event flow\\\\n\\\\n**Critical architecture principle:** Each service maintains its **own PostgreSQL database** (database-per-service pattern). Services do NOT directly access each other's databases. **Kafka acts as the shared event bus** connecting isolated services.\\\\n\\\\n**Complete event flow:**\\\\n\\\\n1. **Service A (Producer)** writes event to its own &#96;outbox_events&#96; table (in postgres-a)\\\\n2. **Kafka Connect CDC** monitors Service A's outbox table via PostgreSQL WAL\\\\n3. **CDC publishes** event to **Kafka topic** (shared infrastructure)\\\\n4. **Service B (Consumer)** subscribes to **Kafka topic**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-b)\\\\n5. **Bridge Service** forwards events from **Kafka → RabbitMQ** (part of standard architecture)\\\\n6. **Service C (Consumer)** subscribes to **RabbitMQ exchange**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-c)\\\\n\\\\n**Result:** Services remain isolated (no shared database), communicate via Kafka, and maintain exactly-once processing guarantees via their own idempotency stores.\\\\n\\\\n### Full Component  │  • SqlAlchemyProcessedMessageStore           │   │\\\\n                │    │  │    (implements IProcessedMessageStore)       │   │\\\\n                │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\\\n                │    │                                                      │\\\\n                │    │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │\\\\n                │    │  │  BRIDGE COMPONENT (Standard Architecture)    │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • BridgeConsumer (Kafka→RabbitMQ bridge)    │   │\\\\n                │    │  │    (manual idempotency, not base class)      │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • Part of dual-broker event distribution    │   │\\\\n                │    │  │                                              │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  RabbitMQ Broker Middleware Stack:           │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • CircuitBreakerMiddleware (resilience)     │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • RateLimiterMiddleware (optional)          │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • RabbitPrometheusMiddleware (metrics)      │   │\\\\n                │    │  │  • TelemetryMiddleware (OTel, shared)        │   │\\\\n                 │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\\\n                 │    │                                  │ On failure        │\\\\n                 │    │                                  ▼                   │\\\\n                 │    │                        ┌──────────────────┐          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │ EXTERNAL DLQ     │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │ (broker native)  │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │ • Kafka Connect  │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │   DLQ SMT        │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │ • RabbitMQ DLX   │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        └─────────┬────────┘          │\\\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\\\n                 │    │                                  ▼                   │\\\\n                 │    │                        ┌──────────────────┐          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │  DLQ Bookkeeper  │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │  Consumer        │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        │  (@broker.sub)   │          │\\\\n                 │    │                        └──────────────────┘          │\\\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\\\n                 └────┼──────────────────────────────────┘                   │\\\\n                      │                                                      │\\\\n                      └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\\\\n                                                 │\\\\n                                                 ▼\\\\n                                    ┌─────────────────────┐\\\\n                                    │  Admin/Ops API      │\\\\n                                    │  • DLQAdminService  │\\\\n                                    │  • Replay API       │\\\\n                                    │  • Health Checks    │\\\\n                                    └─────────────────────┘\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n## FastStream: The Broker Adapter\\\\n\\\\n[FastStream](https://faststream.ag2.ai/) is the **Pythonic adapter** that wraps low-level Kafka/RabbitMQ clients (like &#96;confluent-kafka&#96;, &#96;aio-pika&#96;) and provides a decorator-based, async/await, type-safe API.\\\\n\\\\n**Think of it like SQLAlchemy for message brokers** - it hides 200+ lines of boilerplate:\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\n# WITHOUT FastStream (raw Kafka client)\\\\nfrom confluent_kafka import Consumer\\\\nconsumer = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092', ...})\\\\nconsumer.subscribe(['user.created'])\\\\nwhile True:\\\\n    msg = consumer.poll(1.0)\\\\n    if msg:\\\\n        event_dict = json.loads(msg.value().decode())\\\\n        event = UserCreatedEvent(**event_dict)  # Manual conversion\\\\n        await handle_user_created(event)\\\\n        consumer.commit(msg)\\\\n\\\\n# WITH FastStream (what you actually write)\\\\nfrom faststream.confluent import KafkaBroker\\\\n\\\\nbroker = KafkaBroker(\\\\\\\"localhost:9092\\\\\\\")\\\\n\\\\n@broker.subscriber(\\\\\\\"user.created\\\\\\\")\\\\nasync def handle_user_created(event: UserCreatedEvent):  # ✅ Already a Pydantic model!\\\\n    # FastStream automatically:\\\\n    # - Polls Kafka, decodes bytes → JSON → Pydantic\\\\n    # - Runs middleware (telemetry, circuit breaker, rate limiter)\\\\n    # - Commits offset on success, sends to DLQ on failure\\\\n    print(f\\\\\\\"User {event.user_id} created!\\\\\\\")\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**What FastStream provides:**\\\\n\\\\n- 🎯 **Decorator-based subscriptions**: &#96;@broker.subscriber(\\\\\\\"topic\\\\\\\")&#96;\\\\n- 🔄 **Automatic Pydantic conversion**: Type hints → auto-deserialization\\\\n- 🔌 **Middleware hooks**: Circuit breakers, rate limiting, telemetry, metrics\\\\n- 🏥 **Native health checks**: &#96;make_ping_asgi&#96; endpoint for broker status\\\\n- 🔀 **Unified API**: Same code works for Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS, Redis Streams\\\\n\\\\n&#96;python-eventing&#96; uses FastStream for all Kafka/RabbitMQ interactions, giving you a clean, Pythonic API while handling all the low-level complexity.\\\\n\\\\n## Setup\\\\n\\\\n### Database schema\\\\n\\\\nThe outbox table stores events transactionally with your business data:\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;sql\\\\nCREATE TABLE outbox_events (\\\\n    event_id VARCHAR(36) PRIMARY KEY,\\\\n    event_type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\\\n    aggregate_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\\\n    payload JSONB NOT NULL,\\\\n    occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\\\n    published BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\\\n    failed BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\\\n    attempt_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL,\\\\n    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\\\n    published_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\\\n    failed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\\\n    error_message TEXT\\\\n);\\\\n\\\\nCREATE INDEX idx_outbox_unpublished ON outbox_events (published, created_at);\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n### Application startup\\\\n\\\\nInitialize the outbox repository and event bus at application startup:\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI\\\\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine\\\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\\\nfrom messaging.core import build_event_bus\\\\n\\\\napp = FastAPI()\\\\n\\\\n@app.on_event(\\\\\\\"startup\\\\\\\")\\\\nasync def startup():\\\\n    # Database engine\\\\n    engine = create_async_engine(\\\\\\\"postgresql+asyncpg://...\\\\\\\")\\\\n    \\\\n    # Outbox repository\\\\n    outbox_repo = SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository(engine)\\\\n    app.state.outbox_repository = outbox_repo\\\\n    \\\\n    # Optional: EventBus for advanced patterns\\\\n    event_bus = build_event_bus()\\\\n    app.state.event_bus = event_bus\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**CDC Publishing:** Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC automatically detects outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. See [&#96;debezium-cdc-architecture.md&#96;](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/debezium-cdc-architecture.html) for configuration.\\\\n\\\\n## Quick Start: Transactional Outbox\\\\n\\\\nThe **core pattern** is the transactional outbox - persist events atomically with your business data:\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\nfrom fastapi import Depends\\\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\\\n\\\\n# Define domain event\\\\nclass UserCreated(BaseEvent):\\\\n    event_type: str = \\\\\\\"user.created\\\\\\\"\\\\n    aggregate_id: str\\\\n    user_id: int\\\\n    email: str\\\\n\\\\n# Simple, direct approach (recommended)\\\\n@app.post(\\\\\\\"/users\\\\\\\")\\\\nasync def create_user(\\\\n    data: CreateUserRequest,\\\\n    session = Depends(get_session),\\\\n    outbox_repo: SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository = Depends(get_outbox_repo)\\\\n):\\\\n    # 1. Business logic\\\\n    user = User(**data.dict())\\\\n    session.add(user)\\\\n\\\\n    # 2. Persist event to outbox (same transaction)\\\\n    await outbox_repo.add_event(\\\\n        UserCreated(\\\\n            aggregate_id=f\\\\\\\"user-{user.id}\\\\\\\",\\\\n            user_id=user.id,\\\\n            email=user.email,\\\\n        ),\\\\n        session=session\\\\n    )\\\\n\\\\n    # 3. Commit both atomically\\\\n    await session.commit()\\\\n\\\\n    # 4. Kafka Connect (Debezium CDC) detects the outbox insert and publishes to Kafka\\\\n    return {\\\\\\\"user_id\\\\\\\": user.id}\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**Result**: Guaranteed delivery, no lost events, atomic writes.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## Advanced: EventBus (Optional)\\\\n\\\\nFor **decoupled architectures** with multiple side effects per event, use the **EventBus** abstraction layer:\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import OutboxEventHandler\\\\n\\\\n# Access EventBus (initialized at startup)\\\\nevent_bus = request.app.state.event_bus\\\\noutbox_repo = request.app.state.outbox_repository\\\\n\\\\n# Register handler (typically at startup)\\\\nevent_bus.register(UserCreated, OutboxEventHandler(outbox_repo))\\\\n\\\\n# Dispatch (same result as direct add_event, but decoupled)\\\\nawait event_bus.dispatch(UserCreated(...))\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**When to use EventBus**:\\\\n\\\\n- ✅ Multiple side effects per event (audit, metrics, cache)\\\\n- ✅ Need lifecycle hooks for observability\\\\n- ✅ Testing isolation (enable/disable toggle)\\\\n- ✅ Decorator-based handler registration\\\\n\\\\n**When NOT needed**:\\\\n\\\\n- ❌ Simple event persistence (use direct &#96;outbox_repo.add_event()&#96;)\\\\n- ❌ Single handler per event\\\\n- ❌ No need for hooks/tracing\\\\n\\\\n📚 **[EventBus Documentation](./docs/eventbus/usage-guide.md)** - Complete guide for advanced patterns\\\\n\\\\n## Distribution\\\\n\\\\n- PyPI distribution name: &#96;python-eventing&#96;\\\\n- Python import package: &#96;messaging&#96;\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\\\n# Install\\\\npip install python-eventing\\\\n\\\\n# Import\\\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\nServices should consume the published package rather than source checkout. Kafka remains shared infrastructure with local producer/consumer clients per service.\\\\n\\\\n## Local development\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\\\\npoetry install\\\\npoetry build\\\\npoetry run pytest\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n### Testing\\\\n\\\\n**Local (Windows):**\\\\n- Run non-RabbitMQ tests: &#96;poetry run pytest tests/ -v -m \\\\\\\"not requires_rabbitmq\\\\\\\"&#96;\\\\n- RabbitMQ integration tests fail due to Docker Desktop networking limitations\\\\n\\\\n**CI/CD (Recommended for complete coverage):**\\\\n- GitHub Actions runs all 192 tests on Linux (100% pass rate)\\\\n- Workflows configured in &#96;.github/workflows/&#96;\\\\n- See &#96;.github/workflows/README.md&#96; for details\\\\n\\\\n**For complete local test coverage on Windows:** See [Windows Testing with WSL2](docs/WINDOWS_TESTING_WITH_WSL2.md) (requires Docker Desktop WSL integration setup).\\\\n\\\\n\\\"}\"}],\"structuredContent\":{\"ok\":true,\"path\":\"c:\\\\coding\\\\gridflow-microservices-codex-taskmaster\\\\microservices\\\\eventing\\\\README.md\",\"content\":\"# Eventing\\n\\n[Documentation Status](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest)\\n[Tests](https://github.com/firstunicorn/python-eventing/actions)\\n[Python](https://www.python.org/downloads/)\\n[License](LICENSE)\\n[Code Style](https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff)\\n[Validate Dependencies](https://github.com/firstunicorn/python-eventing/actions/workflows/validate-dependencies.yml)\\n\\n## Table of contents\\n\\n- [Installation](#installation)\\n- [When to use this package](#when-to-use-this-package)\\n- [Comparison with alternatives](#comparison-with-alternatives)\\n- [Scope](#scope)\\n- [Quick start: transactional outbox](#quick-start-transactional-outbox)\\n- [Setup](#setup)\\n- [Advanced: EventBus](#advanced-eventbus-optional)\\n- [Documentation](#documentation)\\n- [Local development](#local-development)\\n\\nPackage-first universal event infrastructure for microservices.\\n\\n📚 **[Full Documentation](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)** - Comprehensive guides and API reference\\n\\n## Installation\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\npip install python-eventing\\n# or\\npoetry add python-eventing\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**Import package name:** &#96;messaging&#96; (distribution is &#96;python-eventing&#96;)\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**Requirements:**\\n- Python 3.12+\\n- PostgreSQL (outbox persistence)\\n- Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC (publishing infrastructure)\\n\\n## When to use this package\\n\\n**Use &#96;python-eventing&#96; if you need:**\\n- Guaranteed event delivery via transactional outbox pattern\\n- Kafka-based microservice messaging with CDC publishing\\n- Dead letter queue handling with database bookkeeping\\n- Idempotent consumer patterns with durable deduplication\\n- Native broker integration (FastStream, Debezium CDC, RabbitMQ DLX)\\n\\n**Consider alternatives if:**\\n- Simple in-process events only → [&#96;pyventus&#96;](https://github.com/mdapena/pyventus)\\n- FastAPI request-scoped events → [&#96;fastapi-events&#96;](https://github.com/melvinkcx/fastapi-events)\\n- Non-Kafka message brokers without CDC support\\n- No need for durable outbox persistence\\n\\nSupport scale: &#96;❌&#96; none, &#96;✅&#96; basic, &#96;✅✅&#96; strong, &#96;✅✅✅&#96; first-class\\n\\n## Comparison with alternatives\\n\\n&#96;python-eventing&#96; prioritizes **durable messaging** (transactional outbox + CDC) and **Kafka/RabbitMQ integration** over in-process event simplicity:\\n\\n| Capability | &#96;python-eventing&#96; | [&#96;pyventus&#96;](https://github.com/mdapena/pyventus) | [&#96;fastapi-events&#96;](https://github.com/melvinkcx/fastapi-events) | Notes |\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n| Transactional outbox | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Durable local DB plus outbox boundary is a core feature here |\\n| Kafka data plane | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | This package is built for Kafka-backed microservice messaging |\\n| DLQ handling | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Leverages native RabbitMQ DLX and Kafka Connect DLQ SMT with database bookkeeping |\\n| Health checks for eventing runtime | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Outbox health checks plus FastStream ASGI broker health endpoint |\\n| Typed cross-service event contracts | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;python-eventing&#96; and &#96;fastapi-events&#96; are stronger on explicit payload modeling |\\n| Decorator subscriber registration | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;EventBus.subscriber(...)&#96; exists now; &#96;pyventus&#96; is still the most polished here |\\n| In-process dispatch backend abstraction | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchBackend&#96; exists here; &#96;pyventus&#96; offers a broader processor model |\\n| Lifecycle hooks / callbacks | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchHooks&#96; covers dispatch, success, failure, disabled, and debug |\\n| Debug / disable controls | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;DispatchSettings(enabled, debug)&#96; is implemented; &#96;fastapi-events&#96; is strongest for app-level toggling |\\n| Observability / telemetry polish | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | FastStream native middlewares (KafkaTelemetryMiddleware, KafkaPrometheusMiddleware) integrated |\\n| Resilience middleware | ✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | CircuitBreakerMiddleware (prevents cascading failures) and RateLimiterMiddleware (throttles consumption rate) |\\n| CDC-based outbox publishing | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC handles outbox-to-Kafka publishing |\\n| Consumer dedup helper | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IdempotentConsumerBase&#96; now uses a durable processed-message store instead of process memory |\\n| Durable cross-service idempotency | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IProcessedMessageStore&#96; plus &#96;SqlAlchemyProcessedMessageStore&#96; provide transactional duplicate protection |\\n| Consumer batch handling | ❌ | ❌ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;fastapi-events&#96; supports &#96;handle_many(...)&#96;; this package stays one-message-per-consume today |\\n| FastAPI-local event flow | ❌ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | This package intentionally avoids request-lifecycle middleware eventing |\\n\\n## Scope\\n\\n**Included:**\\n- Transactional outbox primitives (write-side only; CDC handles publishing)\\n- Event contracts and registry\\n- Kafka/RabbitMQ consumer base classes with idempotency\\n- Native broker integration (Kafka Connect CDC, RabbitMQ DLX, FastStream middlewares)\\n- In-process emitter/subscriber facade and hooks\\n- DLQ bookkeeping consumer for database flag synchronization\\n\\n**NOT included (delegated to external systems):**\\n- Event publishing (handled by Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC)\\n- Message broker infrastructure setup (use official Kafka/RabbitMQ documentation)\\n- Schema registry management (use Confluent Schema Registry or alternatives)\\n- Request-scoped FastAPI event middleware (intentionally avoided)\\n- Consumer batch handling (use &#96;fastapi-events&#96; if needed)\\n\\n## Documentation\\n\\n📖 **[Integration Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/integration-guide.html)** - Step-by-step integration instructions\\n\\n🔍 **[API Reference](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/index.html)** - Complete API documentation\\n\\n📋 **[Event Catalog](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/event-catalog.html)** - Available event types and contracts\\n\\n### Key topics\\n\\n- [Transactional Outbox Pattern](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/transactional-outbox.html) - Guaranteed event delivery (PRIMARY)\\n- [Cross-Service Communication](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html) - Database isolation, Kafka/RabbitMQ architecture, production deployment\\n- [Idempotent Consumers](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/consumer-transactions.html) - Duplicate message handling\\n- [Health Checks](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/eventing/infrastructure/health/index.html) - Monitoring outbox and broker status\\n\\n**Architecture note**: This package handles the **write side** of the outbox pattern (persisting events transactionally with business data). **Publishing** is delegated to Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC, which captures outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. The **bridge component** (part of standard architecture) forwards events from Kafka to RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP. Dead letter handling leverages native broker mechanisms (RabbitMQ DLX, Kafka Connect DLQ SMT) with a minimal bookkeeping consumer to maintain database failed-event flags.\\n\\n## Architecture\\n\\n### Quick Overview\\n\\n**Cross-service communication pattern:** Each microservice has its own PostgreSQL database (database-per-service). Services communicate via **Kafka** (shared event backbone) and **RabbitMQ** (dual-broker pattern via bridge).\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\\nflowchart LR\\n    subgraph Service_A[\\\"Service A (Producer)\\\"]\\n        AppA[FastAPI Route]\\n        DB1[(PostgreSQL A\u003cbr/\u003eoutbox_events)]\\n    end\\n\\n    subgraph Infrastructure[\\\"Shared Infrastructure\\\"]\\n        CDC[Kafka Connect CDC]\\n        Kafka([Apache Kafka\u003cbr/\u003eTopic: events])\\n        Bridge[Eventing Bridge\u003cbr/\u003eKafka → RabbitMQ]\\n        RMQ([RabbitMQ\u003cbr/\u003eExchange: events])\\n    end\\n\\n    subgraph Service_B[\\\"Service B (Kafka Consumer)\\\"]\\n        Handler1[Event Handler]\\n        DB2[(PostgreSQL B\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\n    end\\n\\n    subgraph Service_C[\\\"Service C (RabbitMQ Consumer)\\\"]\\n        Handler2[Event Handler]\\n        DB3[(PostgreSQL C\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\n    end\\n\\n    DLQ([DLQ: Native Broker])\\n\\n    %% Primary Path (Kafka)\\n    AppA --\u003e|1. Atomic Write| DB1\\n    DB1 -.-\u003e|2. CDC Monitor| CDC\\n    CDC --\u003e|3. Publish| Kafka\\n    Kafka --\u003e|4. Subscribe| Handler1\\n    Handler1 --\u003e|5. Dedup Check| DB2\\n\\n    %% Bridge Path (RabbitMQ)\\n    Kafka -.-\u003e|6. Bridge Forward| Bridge\\n    Bridge --\u003e|7. Publish| RMQ\\n    RMQ -.-\u003e|8. Subscribe| Handler2\\n    Handler2 --\u003e|9. Dedup Check| DB3\\n\\n    %% Error Flow\\n    Handler1 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\n    Handler2 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\n\\n    %% Styling\\n    classDef app fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#fff\\n    classDef db fill:#2196F3,stroke:#1565C0,color:#fff\\n    classDef broker fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#fff\\n    classDef infra fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#fff\\n    classDef error fill:#F44336,stroke:#C62828,color:#fff\\n\\n    class AppA,Handler1,Handler2 app\\n    class DB1,DB2,DB3 db\\n    class Kafka,RMQ broker\\n    class CDC,Bridge infra\\n    class DLQ error\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**Key points:**\\n\\n- Each service has **separate PostgreSQL database** (DB1, DB2, DB3)\\n- **Kafka** is the shared event bus connecting all services\\n- **Kafka Connect CDC** watches Service A's outbox and publishes to Kafka\\n- **Service B** consumes directly from Kafka\\n- **Bridge** forwards Kafka → RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP\\n- **Service C** consumes from RabbitMQ\\n- Both Kafka and RabbitMQ are part of the **standard architecture**\\n\\n**Guarantees:**\\n\\n- **Write Phase**: ✅ Atomic (business data + event in same transaction, same database)\\n- **Publish Phase**: ✅ At-least-once (CDC retries on failure)\\n- **Consume Phase**: ✅ Exactly-once (idempotency via processed message store in consumer's database)\\n\\n📖 **[Cross-Service Communication Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html)** - Detailed explanation with production deployment patterns\\n\\n### Database isolation and event flow\\n\\n**Critical architecture principle:** Each service maintains its **own PostgreSQL database** (database-per-service pattern). Services do NOT directly access each other's databases. **Kafka acts as the shared event bus** connecting isolated services.\\n\\n**Complete event flow:**\\n\\n1. **Service A (Producer)** writes event to its own &#96;outbox_events&#96; table (in postgres-a)\\n2. **Kafka Connect CDC** monitors Service A's outbox table via PostgreSQL WAL\\n3. **CDC publishes** event to **Kafka topic** (shared infrastructure)\\n4. **Service B (Consumer)** subscribes to **Kafka topic**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-b)\\n5. **Bridge Service** forwards events from **Kafka → RabbitMQ** (part of standard architecture)\\n6. **Service C (Consumer)** subscribes to **RabbitMQ exchange**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-c)\\n\\n**Result:** Services remain isolated (no shared database), communicate via Kafka, and maintain exactly-once processing guarantees via their own idempotency stores.\\n\\n### Full Component Architecture\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;text\\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\\n│                         YOUR SERVICE (FastAPI)                               │\\n│                                                                              │\\n│  ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │\\n│  │   Routes     │──┬──▶│      python-eventing (SHARED CONTRACTS)       │    │\\n│  └──────────────┘  │   │                                               │    │\\n│                    │   │  • BaseEvent (Pydantic base)                  │    │\\n│                    │   │  • IOutboxRepository (Protocol)               │    │\\n│                    │   │  • IProcessedMessageStore (Protocol)          │    │\\n│                    │   └───────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘    │\\n│                    │                       │ Uses contracts                 │\\n│                    │                       ▼                                │\\n│                    │   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │\\n│                    │   │         python-eventing (implementations)     │    │\\n│                    │   │                                               │    │\\n│                    │   │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │    │\\n│                    │   │  │  WRITE SIDE (Primary)                  │  │    │\\n│                    └───┼──│  • SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository          │  │    │\\n│                        │  │    (implements IOutboxRepository)      │  │    │\\n│                        │  │  • OutboxEventHandler                  │  │    │\\n│                        │  │                                        │  │    │\\n│                        │  │  OPTIONAL (In-process dispatch)        │  │    │\\n│                        │  │  • EventBus (NOT wired to outbox)      │  │    │\\n│                        │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │    │\\n│                        └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │\\n└────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘\\n                                     │ Writes to\\n                                     ▼\\n                          ┌─────────────────────┐\\n                          │   PostgreSQL DB     │\\n                          │                     │\\n                          │ ┌─────────────────┐ │\\n                          │ │ Business Tables │ │\\n                          │ └─────────────────┘ │\\n                          │ ┌─────────────────┐ │◀─┐\\n                          │ │ outbox_event_   │ │  │ Monitors\\n                          │ │ record          │ │  │ (WAL)\\n                          │ └────────┬────────┘ │  │\\n                          │ ┌────────▼────────┐ │  │\\n                          │ │ processed_      │ │  │\\n                          │ │ messages        │ │  │\\n                          │ └─────────────────┘ │  │\\n                          └─────────────────────┘  │\\n                                     │              │\\n                 ┌───────────────────┼──────────────┘\\n                 │                   │\\n                 │                   ▼\\n IProcessedMessageStore)       │   │\\n                │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\n                │    │                                                      │\\n CircuitBreakerMiddleware (resilience)     │   │\\n                │    │  │  • RateLimiterMiddleware (optional)          │   │\\n                │    │  │  • RabbitPrometheusMiddleware (metrics)      │   │\\n                │    │  │  • TelemetryMiddleware (OTel, shared)        │   │\\n                 │    │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\\n                 │    │        │\\n                 │    │                        │ • RabbitMQ DLX   │          │\\n                 │    │                        └─────────┬────────┘          │\\n                 │    │                                  │                   │\\n                 │    │                                  ▼                   │\\n                 │    │                        ┌──────────────────┐          │\\n                 │    │                        │  DLQ Bookkeeper  │          │\\n                 │    │                        │  Consumer        │          │\\n                 │    │                        │  (@broker.sub)   │          │\\n                 │    │                        └──────────────────┘          │\\n                 │    │                     └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\\n                                                 │\\n                                                 ▼\\n                                    ┌─────────────────────┐\\n                                    │  Admin/Ops API      │\\n                                    │  • DLQAdminService  │\\n                                    │  • Replay API       │\\n                                    │  • Health Checks    │\\n                                    └─────────────────────┘\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n## FastStream: The Broker Adapter\\n\\n[FastStream](https://faststream.ag2.ai/) is the **Pythonic adapter** that wraps low-level Kafka/RabbitMQ clients (like &#96;confluent-kafka&#96;, &#96;aio-pika&#96;) and provides a decorator-based, async/await, type-safe API.\\n\\n**Think of it like SQLAlchemy for message brokers** - it hides 200+ lines of boilerplate:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\n# WITHOUT FastStream (raw Kafka client)\\nfrom confluent_kafka import Consumer\\nconsumer = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092', ...})\\nconsumer.subscribe(['user.created'])\\nwhile True:\\n    msg = consumer.poll(1.0)\\n    if msg:\\n        event_dict = json.loads(msg.value().decode())\\n        event = UserCreatedEvent(**event_dict)  # Manual conversion\\n        await handle_user_created(event)\\n        consumer.commit(msg)\\n\\n# WITH FastStream (what you actually write)\\nfrom faststream.confluent import KafkaBroker\\n\\nbroker = KafkaBroker(\\\"localhost:9092\\\")\\n\\n@broker.subscriber(\\\"user.created\\\")\\nasync def handle_user_created(event: UserCreatedEvent):  # ✅ Already a Pydantic model!\\n    # FastStream automatically:\\n    # - Polls Kafka, decodes bytes → JSON → Pydantic\\n    # - Runs middleware (telemetry, circuit breaker, rate limiter)\\n    # - Commits offset on success, sends to DLQ on failure\\n    print(f\\\"User {event.user_id} created!\\\")\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**What FastStream provides:**\\n\\n- 🎯 **Decorator-based subscriptions**: &#96;@broker.subscriber(\\\"topic\\\")&#96;\\n- 🔄 **Automatic Pydantic conversion**: Type hints → auto-deserialization\\n- 🔌 **Middleware hooks**: Circuit breakers, rate limiting, telemetry, metrics\\n- 🏥 **Native health checks**: &#96;make_ping_asgi&#96; endpoint for broker status\\n- 🔀 **Unified API**: Same code works for Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS, Redis Streams\\n\\n&#96;python-eventing&#96; uses FastStream for all Kafka/RabbitMQ interactions, giving you a clean, Pythonic API while handling all the low-level complexity.\\n\\n## Setup\\n\\n### Database schema\\n\\nThe outbox table stores events transactionally with your business data:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;sql\\nCREATE TABLE outbox_events (\\n    event_id VARCHAR(36) PRIMARY KEY,\\n    event_type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\n    aggregate_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,\\n    payload JSONB NOT NULL,\\n    occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\n    published BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\n    failed BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE NOT NULL,\\n    attempt_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL,\\n    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,\\n    published_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\n    failed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,\\n    error_message TEXT\\n);\\n\\nCREATE INDEX idx_outbox_unpublished ON outbox_events (published, created_at);\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Application startup\\n\\nInitialize the outbox repository and event bus at application startup:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI\\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\nfrom messaging.core import build_event_bus\\n\\napp = FastAPI()\\n\\n@app.on_event(\\\"startup\\\")\\nasync def startup():\\n    # Database engine\\n    engine = create_async_engine(\\\"postgresql+asyncpg://...\\\")\\n    \\n    # Outbox repository\\n    outbox_repo = SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository(engine)\\n    app.state.outbox_repository = outbox_repo\\n    \\n    # Optional: EventBus for advanced patterns\\n    event_bus = build_event_bus()\\n    app.state.event_bus = event_bus\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**CDC Publishing:** Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC automatically detects outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. See [&#96;debezium-cdc-architecture.md&#96;](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/debezium-cdc-architecture.html) for configuration.\\n\\n## Quick Start: Transactional Outbox\\n\\nThe **core pattern** is the transactional outbox - persist events atomically with your business data:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom fastapi import Depends\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\n\\n# Define domain event\\nclass UserCreated(BaseEvent):\\n    event_type: str = \\\"user.created\\\"\\n    aggregate_id: str\\n    user_id: int\\n    email: str\\n\\n# Simple, direct approach (recommended)\\n@app.post(\\\"/users\\\")\\nasync def create_user(\\n    data: CreateUserRequest,\\n    session = Depends(get_session),\\n    outbox_repo: SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository = Depends(get_outbox_repo)\\n):\\n    # 1. Business logic\\n    user = User(**data.dict())\\n    session.add(user)\\n\\n    # 2. Persist event to outbox (same transaction)\\n    await outbox_repo.add_event(\\n        UserCreated(\\n            aggregate_id=f\\\"user-{user.id}\\\",\\n            user_id=user.id,\\n            email=user.email,\\n        ),\\n        session=session\\n    )\\n\\n    # 3. Commit both atomically\\n    await session.commit()\\n\\n    # 4. Kafka Connect (Debezium CDC) detects the outbox insert and publishes to Kafka\\n    return {\\\"user_id\\\": user.id}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**Result**: Guaranteed delivery, no lost events, atomic writes.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Advanced: EventBus (Optional)\\n\\nFor **decoupled architectures** with multiple side effects per event, use the **EventBus** abstraction layer:\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import OutboxEventHandler\\n\\n# Access EventBus (initialized at startup)\\nevent_bus = request.app.state.event_bus\\noutbox_repo = request.app.state.outbox_repository\\n\\n# Register handler (typically at startup)\\nevent_bus.register(UserCreated, OutboxEventHandler(outbox_repo))\\n\\n# Dispatch (same result as direct add_event, but decoupled)\\nawait event_bus.dispatch(UserCreated(...))\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n**When to use EventBus**:\\n\\n- ✅ Multiple side effects per event (audit, metrics, cache)\\n- ✅ Need lifecycle hooks for observability\\n- ✅ Testing isolation (enable/disable toggle)\\n- ✅ Decorator-based handler registration\\n\\n**When NOT needed**:\\n\\n- ❌ Simple event persistence (use direct &#96;outbox_repo.add_event()&#96;)\\n- ❌ Single handler per event\\n- ❌ No need for hooks/tracing\\n\\n📚 **[EventBus Documentation](./docs/eventbus/usage-guide.md)** - Complete guide for advanced patterns\\n\\n## Distribution\\n\\n- PyPI distribution name: &#96;python-eventing&#96;\\n- Python import package: &#96;messaging&#96;\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\\n# Install\\npip install python-eventing\\n\\n# Import\\nfrom messaging.core import BaseEvent\\nfrom messaging.infrastructure import SqlAlchemyOutboxRepository\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\nServices should consume the published package rather than source checkout. Kafka remains shared infrastructure with local producer/consumer clients per service.\\n\\n## Local development\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;powershell\\npoetry install\\npoetry build\\npoetry run pytest\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Testing\\n\\n**Local (Windows):**\\n- Run non-RabbitMQ tests: &#96;poetry run pytest tests/ -v -m \\\"not requires_rabbitmq\\\"&#96;\\n- RabbitMQ integration tests fail due to Docker Desktop networking limitations\\n\\n**CI/CD (Recommended for complete coverage):**\\n- GitHub Actions runs all 192 tests on Linux (100% pass rate)\\n- Workflows configured in &#96;.github/workflows/&#96;\\n- See &#96;.github/workflows/README.md&#96; for details\\n\\n**For complete local test coverage on Windows:** See [Windows Testing with WSL2](docs/WINDOWS_TESTING_WITH_WSL2.md) (requires Docker Desktop WSL integration setup).\\n\\n\"}}"
}

| [&#96;pyventus&#96;](https://github.com/mdapena/pyventus) | [&#96;fastapi-events&#96;](https://github.com/melvinkcx/fastapi-events) | Notes |\\\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\\\\n| Transactional outbox | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Durable local DB plus outbox boundary is a core feature here |\\\\n| Kafka data plane | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | This package is built for Kafka-backed microservice messaging |\\\\n| DLQ handling | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Leverages native RabbitMQ DLX and Kafka Connect DLQ SMT with database bookkeeping |\\\\n| Health checks for eventing runtime | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Outbox health checks plus FastStream ASGI broker health endpoint |\\\\n| Typed cross-service event contracts | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;python-eventing&#96; and &#96;fastapi-events&#96; are stronger on explicit payload modeling |\\\\n| Decorator subscriber registration | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅✅ | &#96;EventBus.subscriber(...)&#96; exists now; &#96;pyventus&#96; is still the most polished here |\\\\n| In-process dispatch backend abstraction | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchBackend&#96; exists here; &#96;pyventus&#96; offers a broader processor model |\\\\n| Lifecycle hooks / callbacks | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | &#96;DispatchHooks&#96; covers dispatch, success, failure, disabled, and debug |\\\\n| Debug / disable controls | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;DispatchSettings(enabled, debug)&#96; is implemented; &#96;fastapi-events&#96; is strongest for app-level toggling |\\\\n| Observability / telemetry polish | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | FastStream native middlewares (KafkaTelemetryMiddleware, KafkaPrometheusMiddleware) integrated |\\\\n| Resilience middleware | ✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | CircuitBreakerMiddleware (prevents cascading failures) and RateLimiterMiddleware (throttles consumption rate) |\\\\n| CDC-based outbox publishing | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC handles outbox-to-Kafka publishing |\\\\n| Consumer dedup helper | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IdempotentConsumerBase&#96; now uses a durable processed-message store instead of process memory |\\\\n| Durable cross-service idempotency | ✅✅✅ | ❌ | ❌ | &#96;IProcessedMessageStore&#96; plus &#96;SqlAlchemyProcessedMessageStore&#96; provide transactional duplicate protection |\\\\n| Consumer batch handling | ❌ | ❌ | ✅✅✅ | &#96;fastapi-events&#96; supports &#96;handle_many(...)&#96;; this package stays one-message-per-consume today |\\\\n| FastAPI-local event flow | ❌ | ✅ | ✅✅✅ | This package intentionally avoids request-lifecycle middleware eventing |\\\\n\\\\n## Scope\\\\n\\\\n**Included:**\\\\n- Transactional outbox primitives (write-side only; CDC handles publishing)\\\\n- Event contracts and registry\\\\n- Kafka/RabbitMQ consumer base classes with idempotency\\\\n- Native broker integration (Kafka Connect CDC, RabbitMQ DLX, FastStream middlewares)\\\\n- In-process emitter/subscriber facade and hooks\\\\n- DLQ bookkeeping consumer for database flag synchronization\\\\n\\\\n**NOT included (delegated to external systems):**\\\\n- Event publishing (handled by Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC)\\\\n- Message broker infrastructure setup (use official Kafka/RabbitMQ documentation)\\\\n- Schema registry management (use Confluent Schema Registry or alternatives)\\\\n- Request-scoped FastAPI event middleware (intentionally avoided)\\\\n- Consumer batch handling (use &#96;fastapi-events&#96; if needed)\\\\n\\\\n## Documentation\\\\n\\\\n📖 **[Integration Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/integration-guide.html)** - Step-by-step integration instructions\\\\n\\\\n🔍 **[API Reference](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/index.html)** - Complete API documentation\\\\n\\\\n📋 **[Event Catalog](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/event-catalog.html)** - Available event types and contracts\\\\n\\\\n### Key topics\\\\n\\\\n- [Transactional Outbox Pattern](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/transactional-outbox.html) - Guaranteed event delivery (PRIMARY)\\\\n- [Cross-Service Communication](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html) - Database isolation, Kafka/RabbitMQ architecture, production deployment\\\\n- [Idempotent Consumers](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/consumer-transactions.html) - Duplicate message handling\\\\n- [Health Checks](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/autoapi/eventing/infrastructure/health/index.html) - Monitoring outbox and broker status\\\\n\\\\n**Architecture note**: This package handles the **write side** of the outbox pattern (persisting events transactionally with business data). **Publishing** is delegated to Kafka Connect with Debezium CDC, which captures outbox table changes and publishes to Kafka. The **bridge component** (part of standard architecture) forwards events from Kafka to RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP. Dead letter handling leverages native broker mechanisms (RabbitMQ DLX, Kafka Connect DLQ SMT) with a minimal bookkeeping consumer to maintain database failed-event flags.\\\\n\\\\n## Architecture\\\\n\\\\n### Quick Overview\\\\n\\\\n**Cross-service communication pattern:** Each microservice has its own PostgreSQL database (database-per-service). Services communicate via **Kafka** (shared event backbone) and **RabbitMQ** (dual-broker pattern via bridge).\\\\n\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\\\\nflowchart LR\\\\n    subgraph Service_A[\\\\\\\"Service A (Producer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        AppA[FastAPI Route]\\\\n        DB1[(PostgreSQL A\u003cbr/\u003eoutbox_events)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Infrastructure[\\\\\\\"Shared Infrastructure\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        CDC[Kafka Connect CDC]\\\\n        Kafka([Apache Kafka\u003cbr/\u003eTopic: events])\\\\n        Bridge[Eventing Bridge\u003cbr/\u003eKafka → RabbitMQ]\\\\n        RMQ([RabbitMQ\u003cbr/\u003eExchange: events])\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Service_B[\\\\\\\"Service B (Kafka Consumer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        Handler1[Event Handler]\\\\n        DB2[(PostgreSQL B\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    subgraph Service_C[\\\\\\\"Service C (RabbitMQ Consumer)\\\\\\\"]\\\\n        Handler2[Event Handler]\\\\n        DB3[(PostgreSQL C\u003cbr/\u003eprocessed_messages)]\\\\n    end\\\\n\\\\n    DLQ([DLQ: Native Broker])\\\\n\\\\n    %% Primary Path (Kafka)\\\\n    AppA --\u003e|1. Atomic Write| DB1\\\\n    DB1 -.-\u003e|2. CDC Monitor| CDC\\\\n    CDC --\u003e|3. Publish| Kafka\\\\n    Kafka --\u003e|4. Subscribe| Handler1\\\\n    Handler1 --\u003e|5. Dedup Check| DB2\\\\n\\\\n    %% Bridge Path (RabbitMQ)\\\\n    Kafka -.-\u003e|6. Bridge Forward| Bridge\\\\n    Bridge --\u003e|7. Publish| RMQ\\\\n    RMQ -.-\u003e|8. Subscribe| Handler2\\\\n    Handler2 --\u003e|9. Dedup Check| DB3\\\\n\\\\n    %% Error Flow\\\\n    Handler1 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\\\n    Handler2 -.-\u003e|Exception| DLQ\\\\n\\\\n    %% Styling\\\\n    classDef app fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef db fill:#2196F3,stroke:#1565C0,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef broker fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef infra fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#fff\\\\n    classDef error fill:#F44336,stroke:#C62828,color:#fff\\\\n\\\\n    class AppA,Handler1,Handler2 app\\\\n    class DB1,DB2,DB3 db\\\\n    class Kafka,RMQ broker\\\\n    class CDC,Bridge infra\\\\n    class DLQ error\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\n\\\\n**Key points:**\\\\n\\\\n- Each service has **separate PostgreSQL database** (DB1, DB2, DB3)\\\\n- **Kafka** is the shared event bus connecting all services\\\\n- **Kafka Connect CDC** watches Service A's outbox and publishes to Kafka\\\\n- **Service B** consumes directly from Kafka\\\\n- **Bridge** forwards Kafka → RabbitMQ for services preferring AMQP\\\\n- **Service C** consumes from RabbitMQ\\\\n- Both Kafka and RabbitMQ are part of the **standard architecture**\\\\n\\\\n**Guarantees:**\\\\n\\\\n- **Write Phase**: ✅ Atomic (business data + event in same transaction, same database)\\\\n- **Publish Phase**: ✅ At-least-once (CDC retries on failure)\\\\n- **Consume Phase**: ✅ Exactly-once (idempotency via processed message store in consumer's database)\\\\n\\\\n📖 **[Cross-Service Communication Guide](https://python-eventing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cross-service-communication.html)** - Detailed explanation with production deployment patterns\\\\n\\\\n### Database isolation and event flow\\\\n\\\\n**Critical architecture principle:** Each service maintains its **own PostgreSQL database** (database-per-service pattern). Services do NOT directly access each other's databases. **Kafka acts as the shared event bus** connecting isolated services.\\\\n\\\\n**Complete event flow:**\\\\n\\\\n1. **Service A (Producer)** writes event to its own &#96;outbox_events&#96; table (in postgres-a)\\\\n2. **Kafka Connect CDC** monitors Service A's outbox table via PostgreSQL WAL\\\\n3. **CDC publishes** event to **Kafka topic** (shared infrastructure)\\\\n4. **Service B (Consumer)** subscribes to **Kafka topic**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-b)\\\\n5. **Bridge Service** forwards events from **Kafka → RabbitMQ** (part of standard architecture)\\\\n6. **Service C (Consumer)** subscribes to **RabbitMQ exchange**, processes event, stores idempotency check in its own &#96;processed_messages&#96; table (in postgres-c)\\\\n\\\\n**Result:** Services remain isolated (no shared database), communicate via Kafka, and maintain exactly-once processing guarantees via their own idempotency stores.\\\\n\\\\n### Full Component
